segments.title: Leadership across three Russo-Swedish wars
segments.summary: The opening compares the Great Northern War with the wars of 1741–43 and 1788–90, arguing that leadership quality repeatedly shaped Swedish and Russian results.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames the period through three wars and the political intervals between them. He treats the Great Northern War as a coalition victory rather than a simple Russian triumph, calls the 1741–43 leadership poor on both sides, and argues that Sweden won the 1788–90 contest on points. The comparison establishes the lecture's central claim: changes in rulers and commanders mattered as much as national resources.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the three wars and compares their outcomes and leadership.

segments.title: Peter the Great builds a navy around personal rule
segments.summary: Peter I founded the Russian Navy and modernized the state, but many of his institutions depended dangerously on a ruler of his own energy and authority.
segments.body: The lecture credits Peter with founding a navy that became critical to Russia's Baltic wars and with creating new governing institutions. It also stresses the weakness of systems designed around one exceptional individual: mechanisms such as the Table of Ranks could harden into stratification under less capable successors. That tension between institutional reform and personalized rule becomes a recurring explanation for later Russian instability.

evidence.note: Peter's naval foundation, reforms, and dependence on personal authority are discussed.

segments.title: Narva overturns Peter's assumptions about Charles XII
segments.summary: Russia attacked Sweden expecting a young king to be weak, only for Charles XII to inflict a devastating defeat at Narva and reject an early peace.
segments.body: Peter's calculation rested on the assumption that Charles XII's youth meant vulnerability. At Narva, a much smaller Swedish relief force broke the Russian siege army, captured senior officers, and produced mass surrender and heavy losses. Charles then refused Peter's peace approach, revealing both his confidence and the uncompromising strategic outlook that would later impose severe costs on Sweden.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Narva's imbalance, Russian losses, and Charles XII's refusal to end the war.

segments.title: Køge Bay turns an indecisive clash into strategic interdiction
segments.summary: Weather left the Swedish and Danish-Norwegian fleets without a clear tactical result, but Sweden's destruction of the invasion convoy stopped Russian troops reaching Danzig.
segments.body: Hans Wachtmeister brought a smaller Swedish battle fleet against a larger Danish-Norwegian force south of Copenhagen. Groundings and an exploding Danish ship made the fleet action costly without producing a clean tactical decision. The strategic result was clearer: Sweden destroyed the Dutch-Danish convoy intended to carry a Russian invasion force to Danzig. This passage shows why convoy survival and troop movement can matter more than possession of the immediate battle area.

evidence.note: The fleet strengths, losses, groundings, and destruction of the Danzig invasion convoy are described.

segments.title: At Hogland, command pressure drives Russian ships into the shallows
segments.summary: A small Swedish squadron escaped a stronger Russian force by using shoal water, while Peter I's demand for victory encouraged heavy ships to pursue where frigates were better suited.
segments.body: Carl Raab's Swedish squadron faced twice as many Russian ships of the line plus supporting frigates and brigs. His flagship grounded, freed itself, and rejoined the fight, but three pursuing Russian ships followed into the same danger and one had to be burned. Dr. Clarke links the mistake to Peter I's expectation that officers both try hard and win, a pressure that rewarded over-pursuit. The tactical lesson is that shallow-water pursuit should have fallen to frigates rather than ships of the line whose draft exposed them to grounding.

evidence.note: The transcript covers the force imbalance, Swedish grounding and recovery, Russian losses in the shallows, and Peter's command pressure.

segments.title: Gangut rewards Russian concentration after a readiness warning
segments.summary: Russia withdrew from Swedish ships of the line, returned with overwhelming galley strength, and isolated nine coastal vessels after Peter I had criticized fleet maintenance.
segments.body: The Russian force initially declined battle when sixteen Swedish ships of the line protected the area. It returned after the heavy ships departed and committed twenty-three galleys against a Swedish pram, six galleys, and two smaller craft, with many more Russian vessels in reserve. Peter I had recently inspected the Baltic Fleet, condemned its maintenance, and required a substantial artillery advantage before engaging. Gangut was therefore an important Russian victory built on concentration, patience, and favorable local strength rather than an evenly matched tactical contest.

evidence.note: The transcript gives the Swedish and Russian forces, the earlier withdrawal, Peter's readiness inspection, and the concentration at Gangut.

segments.title: Danish-Norwegian pressure exposes Sweden's dependence on central command
segments.summary: Larger Danish-Norwegian forces captured grounded Swedish ships and opened the way to Rügen as Charles XII's absence weakened strategic coordination at home.
segments.body: One Danish victory followed from a two-to-one advantage and Swedish ships grounding near shore rather than being defeated cleanly under sail. At Rügen, another larger Danish-Norwegian fleet forced the Swedes aside and enabled troop transports to support the island campaign, even though changes of command limited Danish tactical fluency. Dr. Clarke uses the sequence to show Sweden's performance deteriorating while Charles XII was isolated from the political center. In a system dependent on personal royal direction, campaigning far away and unreliable communications left fleets without coherent priorities or adequate reinforcement.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Danish numerical advantages, Swedish groundings, the Rügen transports, and weakening central direction.

segments.title: Ösel and Grengam punish Sweden's penny-packet deployments
segments.summary: After Charles XII's death, Swedish leaders sent small squadrons against much larger Russian forces because political pressure favored visible action over concentrated force.
segments.body: At Ösel in 1719, Sweden dispatched one ship of the line, a frigate, and a brigantine against six Russian ships of the line rather than wait for reinforcements. At Grengam in 1720, another small Swedish force faced sixty-one galleys and numerous supporting craft, losing four frigates even as Swedish accounts claimed heavy Russian galley damage. The lecture traces these imbalances to factional government and pressure to be seen acting after Charles XII's death. A stronger concentration might have exploited Swedish seamanship, but fragmented political authority repeatedly produced forces too small for the assigned mission.

evidence.note: The transcript compares the Ösel and Grengam forces and links their deployment to political pressure after Charles XII's death.

segments.title: Charles XII wins battles but neglects strategic depth
segments.summary: Charles XII's operational brilliance could not compensate for an unsupported march toward Moscow, depleted manpower, weak deputies, and an unsettled succession.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes winning battles from building a sustainable war effort. Charles repeatedly defeated larger armies, but failed to retrench, rebuild resources, secure his rear, or create a succession system that could survive his absence. Russia could keep fielding new armies while Sweden's smaller population and less capable subordinate command could not replace accumulated losses.

evidence.note: The transcript connects the Moscow campaign to logistics, manpower, deputies, and succession.

segments.title: Peter's succession problem and the warm-water-port idea
segments.summary: Peter left a fragile succession, while later claims about a sweeping Russian conquest plan obscure the narrower strategic value of reliable maritime access.
segments.body: The discussion moves from Peter's failure to secure an orderly dynasty to the disputed idea of a timeless Russian drive for warm-water ports. Dr. Clarke rejects a later forged geopolitical testament as evidence of a global plan, while accepting that access through the Black Sea or other ice-free routes had real strategic value. He emphasizes that eighteenth-century logistics sharply limited what Russia could actually project toward Constantinople, Afghanistan, or India.

evidence.note: Peter's heirs and the meaning and limits of warm-water access are examined.

segments.title: Russia's succession carousel after Peter I
segments.summary: Catherine I, the child emperor Peter II, and Empress Anna reveal how regencies, favorites, and court factions repeatedly destabilized post-Petrine Russia.
segments.body: Catherine I spent her brief reign protecting Peter's legacy and named his grandson as heir, but Peter II died at fourteen after a government dominated by favorites. Anna brought two decades of provincial ruling experience and built a loyal faction, military education, scientific institutions, and a political-investigation office. Yet her reliance on favorites and her choice of an infant successor failed to create a durable settlement.

evidence.note: The transcript follows Catherine I, Peter II, and Anna through recurring succession failures.

segments.title: Elizabeth turns a coup into controlled stability
segments.summary: After Ivan VI's infant reign, Elizabeth seized power with military and foreign support, combined reform with quiet coercion, and carefully managed her heirs.
segments.body: Ivan VI's brief reign and long imprisonment illustrate the human cost of Russia's unresolved succession. Elizabeth then used army backing and support from French and Swedish diplomats to take the throne. Her popularity rested on education, construction, and a public refusal to execute subjects, although Dr. Clarke cautions that political opponents could still disappear without formal spectacle. She also brought her nephew Peter to Russia, selected his bride Catherine, and personally controlled the upbringing of their son Paul.

evidence.note: Ivan VI's fate, Elizabeth's coup, governing methods, and heir management are described.

segments.title: Swedish naval effort founders on army inaction in 1741–43
segments.summary: The Swedish fleet repeatedly sought combined operations, but an inactive army surrendered the initiative to Russia and exposed failures in joint command.
segments.body: The 1741–43 war centered on Finland and was driven partly by the politics surrounding Elizabeth's seizure of power. Swedish naval forces tried to support amphibious operations, but their army failed to coordinate or act decisively while Field Marshal Lacy kept the Russian side moving. The unsuccessful galley action at the narrows and the prosecution of hesitant commanders show how doctrine, initiative, and civil-military expectations pulled in different directions.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts Swedish naval activity with army inaction and Russian initiative.

segments.title: Hats, Caps, and a deliberately weak Swedish crown
segments.summary: Sweden's rival Hats and Caps factions pursued different economic and war policies while choosing Adolf Frederick precisely because parliament expected to control him.
segments.body: The Hats favored mercantilism and repeated attempts to recover power through war, while the Caps represented clergy and peasant interests, freer trade, and press reform. Adolf Frederick was selected as heir by politicians who valued his weakness and hoped his Prussian marriage would improve relations with Empress Elizabeth. His later attempts to reclaim authority nearly cost him the throne because he could not build or manage a durable coalition. This political structure explains why Swedish strategy could swing with factional priorities and why rulers struggled to coordinate sustained military policy.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the Hats and Caps, Adolf Frederick's selection, and his failed efforts to recover royal power.

segments.title: Peter III proposes reforms without building support
segments.summary: Peter III advanced major reforms but alienated the secret police, church, army, and nobility in only 186 days because he never assembled the coalition needed to carry them through.
segments.body: Peter proposed ending compulsory noble military service, secularizing church lands, creating a state bank, and widening religious equality. Each idea threatened an institution whose cooperation he needed, yet he moved from proposal to proposal without explaining the sequence or protecting his position. His enthusiasm for Prussia deepened suspicion among Russians already wary of Baltic German influence. Catherine's coup succeeded because intelligence and political skill matter as much as a reform program in a personalized state.

evidence.note: The transcript lists Peter III's reforms, the constituencies he alienated, his Prussian policy, and Catherine's overthrow.

segments.title: Catherine II converts political skill into strategic depth
segments.summary: Catherine expanded Russia, chose capable commanders, protected maritime trade, and invested in infrastructure that gave her fleets greater endurance in the later war with Sweden.
segments.body: Catherine's political discipline contrasted with Peter III's inability to anticipate opposition, and she presented herself successfully as a Russian ruler despite her Prussian origins. Her reign added extensive territory around the Black Sea and farther west while relying on capable generals and admirals. Russia still depended on exports, imports, and shipping because land infrastructure could not replace maritime movement, making relations with Britain and access to sea routes strategically important. Investment in bases and support systems later allowed Russian fleets to repair, reinforce, and outlast Swedish forces during the war of 1788–90.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Catherine's political method, territorial expansion, commanders, trade dependence, diplomacy, and infrastructure investment.

segments.title: Gustav III uses war to reinforce enlightened absolutism
segments.summary: Gustav III curbed noble and parliamentary power, widened formal participation, and launched war with Russia while consolidating his own authority.
segments.body: Gustav's 1772 coup restored royal power, and the 1789 Union and Security Act weakened noble privilege while concentrating decision-making in the crown. Dr. Clarke uses Gustav to explain the contradiction of enlightened absolutism: liberty, culture, and reform were encouraged, but the monarch alone was trusted to direct them. The 1788 war offered both a chance to recover Baltic influence and a political setting in which domestic opposition could be controlled.

evidence.note: Gustav's coup, political program, character, and decision for war are discussed.

segments.title: Hogland checks Sweden's landing plan
segments.summary: Sweden fought well in the gunnery exchange at Hogland but failed to exploit it, abandoned its landing plan, and carried fever into Sveaborg aboard a prize.
segments.body: The fleets exchanged one captured ship of the line apiece, and Swedish gunnery put several Russian ships out of action. Yet Sweden could not carry out the intended advance and landing deep in the Gulf of Finland, so the fleet retired to fortified Sveaborg for repair. Disease aboard the captured Russian ship then weakened the Swedish base, helping Admiral Greig secure operational control of the gulf despite the indecisive tactical result.

evidence.note: Fleet composition, the artillery exchange, failed landing, withdrawal, and fever are described.

segments.title: What kept Swedish forces competitive with Russia?
segments.summary: Sweden's edge came less from superior technology than from greater sailing practice, while Catherine's infrastructure investment made the Russian fleet increasingly ready and durable.
segments.body: The two navies were broadly comparable in naval technology. Swedish crews gained an advantage from more frequent sailing and the practical competence that came with it. That edge was not permanent because Catherine II had invested in Russian bases and support systems. The answer helps separate human proficiency from equipment and explains why operational readiness could shift even when technology remained similar.
segments.question: What kept the Swedish military ahead of Russia in tactics or technology?
segments.answerShort: Naval technology was roughly equal, but Swedish crews had more sailing practice; Catherine's infrastructure investment was steadily making the Russian fleet more ready.

evidence.note: The question and answer distinguish Swedish sailing practice from technology and note Russian infrastructure investment.

segments.title: At Öland, supply and heavy guns favor Russia
segments.summary: Nominally equal fleets at Öland were not equal in readiness, supplies, or weight of broadside, forcing Sweden to withdraw after days of maneuver.
segments.body: Fever and slow fitting-out left the Swedish fleet late and poorly prepared. Although each side fielded roughly the same number of ships, Russia possessed more 74-gun ships and three 100-gun first rates while Sweden relied mainly on 64s and 76s. A Swedish victory would have required aggressive concentration and considerable risk; instead dwindling supplies ended the maneuvering contest and produced a Russian operational victory.

evidence.note: The transcript compares readiness, supply, ship counts, and Russian weight of fire.

segments.title: How could an Age of Sail warship be disinfected?
segments.summary: Lime and limewash provided a practical period method for cleaning ships after disease, alongside separating sick crews from the vessel and population.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke identifies lime, particularly limewash, as a method used to clean ships in the period. The answer follows his discussion of fever weakening the Swedish fleet through the winter. Disinfection was not only a medical concern: an infected crew delayed fitting out and could decide whether a fleet reached sea in time for a campaign. The passage therefore connects ship maintenance, public health, and operational readiness.
segments.question: How did navies disinfect ships at the time?
segments.answerShort: They could use lime or limewash to clean the ship, while isolating affected crews helped the illness run its course away from the vessel and population.

evidence.note: The transcript proposes separating crews and disinfecting ships, then answers that lime or limewash was used.

segments.title: Why the eighteenth-century Russian Navy was professional
segments.summary: The imperial navy attracted career-minded officers, including long-serving Scots and Germans, before naval service became a fashionable route for Russian nobles.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke warns against projecting the weaknesses of the Russo-Japanese or Soviet fleets backward onto the eighteenth century. Naval service had not yet become a prestigious destination for politically connected nobles, so many officers were committed professionals seeking advancement. Foreign-born personnel often spent their whole careers in Russian service rather than arriving as temporary advisers from another navy.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Russian naval professionalism and foreign-born career officers.

segments.title: Was Charles XII greater than Gustavus Adolphus?
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke places Charles XII and Gustavus Adolphus broadly on the same level, with Charles stronger in some aspects of command rather than clearly greater overall.
segments.body: The answer is deliberately comparative rather than absolute. Dr. Clarke has already shown Charles XII defeating much larger forces through exceptional battlefield command. He has also emphasized the Moscow campaign's logistical strain and Charles's failure to secure succession or strategic depth. Placing him broadly on a par with Gustavus Adolphus recognizes that tactical brilliance without erasing those wider weaknesses.
segments.question: Do you think Charles XII was greater than Gustavus Adolphus?
segments.answerShort: They were broadly on a par; Charles XII was better in some respects, but not clearly greater overall.

evidence.note: The audience question asks for a comparison and the answer places the two rulers broadly on a par.

segments.title: Incomplete defenses doom Sweden at the first Svensksund
segments.summary: Sweden's plan to seal the narrow approaches was under-resourced and unfinished, allowing a larger Russian force to break through and turn withdrawal into defeat.
segments.body: The Swedish archipelago fleet faced roughly 12,000 Russians with about 5,000 men and tried to compensate by obstructing the channels. Gustav III disliked the defensive concept and did not provide enough support, while critical passages were left open on the mistaken belief that they were unnavigable. Once Russia forced a route through, Sweden had to conduct a costly fighting withdrawal.

evidence.note: The transcript covers the incomplete blockade, force imbalance, and Gustav's resource decisions.

segments.title: Anchored Russian defenses defeat superior Swedish numbers at Reval
segments.summary: Twenty-two Swedish ships attacked ten Russians in a protected anchorage, but poor timing and formation squandered Sweden's numerical advantage.
segments.body: The Russians anchored where shore protection and stable gunnery forced Sweden to attack on unfavorable terms. Strong winds made Swedish fire less effective, but Dr. Clarke treats weather as an inadequate excuse because Prince Charles could have waited or used his numbers differently. The loss and capture of ships reduced Sweden's line-of-battle strength while Russian bases enabled rapid reinforcement and repair.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the anchored defense, weather, fleet imbalance, and Swedish losses.

segments.title: Gustav wins at Frederikshamn but misses the larger prize
segments.summary: Gustav III personally won with the archipelago fleet, yet delay and a granted respite allowed the Russians to reinforce the fort and deny Sweden a decisive capture.
segments.body: Frustrated by Prince Charles, Gustav took direct command and used a somewhat stronger coastal fleet effectively. The victory did not deliver its full strategic promise: an earlier attack might have caught the fort undermanned, and a pause granted to the Russian commander allowed reinforcements to arrive. The episode shows Gustav's energy alongside a recurring inability to turn tactical success into the most valuable objective.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Gustav's command, victory, delay, and missed capture of the fort.

segments.title: Prince Charles cannot convert numbers into victory
segments.summary: Near Kronstadt, Sweden had more ships but lighter guns and a commander unwilling to accept the calculated losses needed to press the attack.
segments.body: The Russian commander only needed to survive until nearby squadrons and bases shifted the balance decisively. Prince Charles withdrew when he recognized that danger, a choice made understandable by Russia's heavier first rates but damaging by his consistent reluctance to take risk. Dr. Clarke argues that numerical advantage alone was insufficient without an aggressive plan for concentrating smaller Swedish ships against heavier opponents.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts Swedish numbers, Russian firepower, and Prince Charles's aversion to calculated losses.

segments.title: At Björkösund, geography defeats superior Russian numbers
segments.summary: Forty-two Swedish gun sloops and boats held a narrow approach against roughly eighty-one Russian vessels by forcing the larger force through a confined killing ground.
segments.body: The Swedish archipelago detachment chose a narrow position through which the Russian force had to advance. Russian frigates, xebecs, floating batteries, and smaller craft attacked for about five hours but could not deploy their numerical advantage effectively. The Swedes then withdrew in order to the main force at Vyborg Bay after inflicting losses and stopping the passage. Björkösund shows how prepared coastal defense and local geography could offset disparities that were decisive in open water.

evidence.note: The transcript gives the forces, narrow defensive position, five-hour attack, Russian losses, and Swedish withdrawal to Vyborg Bay.

segments.title: The Vyborg Bay breakout preserves Sweden's fleet
segments.summary: Gustav's failed approach toward St Petersburg trapped the Swedish fleet, but its breakout denied Russia the decisive destruction it sought.
segments.body: The fleet's predicament followed Gustav's overambitious plan to land near St Petersburg. Russia held the bay and could claim the field, but Sweden's operational purpose became escape rather than possession. Most Swedish warships reached Sveaborg for repairs and the archipelago force reached Svensksund, so preserving the fleet in being set conditions for the war's final battle.

evidence.note: The transcript evaluates the breakout by its objective of saving the Swedish fleet.

segments.title: Second Svensksund restores Sweden's political independence
segments.summary: Sweden's archipelago fleet shattered a larger Russian attack at Svensksund, creating the leverage for a peace that ended Russian interference in Swedish sovereignty.
segments.body: Gustav III commanded a defensive battle in waters suited to Sweden's coastal force. The Russians brought nearly 300 vessels and about 19,000 personnel but lost roughly a quarter to a third of their ships and suffered around 10,000 killed, wounded, or captured. The Treaty of Värälä changed no borders, yet removed Russia's ability to dominate Swedish internal affairs; Dr. Clarke therefore treats the war as a meaningful Swedish success.

evidence.note: The transcript gives the battle's scale, Russian losses, and political consequences at Värälä.

segments.title: Why Peter and Catherine's marriage date matters
segments.summary: An audience question explores why uncertainty over Peter I and Catherine I's early marriage could affect their children's legitimacy and dynastic rights.
segments.body: The surviving historical account includes a later public ceremony but leaves uncertainty over whether an earlier secret marriage occurred before their children were born. That ambiguity matters because legitimacy shaped eligibility for the throne. Dr. Clarke notes that the earlier marriage may have been retrospectively regularized in the record.
segments.question: Is uncertainty about whether Peter I and Catherine I were already married normal for this period, and why does it matter?
segments.answerShort: A later public marriage is documented, but the earlier secret marriage is uncertain; without it, their children's legitimacy and succession rights could be challenged.

evidence.note: The question and answer discuss the uncertain secret marriage and dynastic legitimacy.

segments.title: Could Russia have fought differently at second Svensksund?
segments.summary: The narrow approaches left Russia few good naval options; withdrawal or a coordinated land attack offered better prospects than forcing the defended channels.
segments.body: The fleets were too evenly matched for Russia to overwhelm the Swedish position through restricted water. Even shallow craft had little room to maneuver or develop an attack. A blockade combined with troops landed nearby might have pressured the defense from land, but Russia did not attempt that alternative.
segments.question: Could the Russian fleet have done something different at the second battle of Svensksund?
segments.answerShort: Not much at sea: withdrawal was sensible, while a blockade and land attack might have been the only promising alternative to forcing the narrow channels.

evidence.note: The answer assesses withdrawal, a possible land attack, and limits imposed by the narrows.

segments.title: Could an uninjured Charles XII have taken Moscow?
segments.summary: Even with Charles XII uninjured, Russia's depth and population made a lasting capture of Moscow improbable for Sweden's small army.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke allows that Charles might have reached or bombarded Moscow and then withdrawn. A siege was far less plausible because Russian armies could keep arriving from multiple directions while Sweden could not replace losses at the same rate. Sweden's chronic manpower shortage, partly offset by artillery and disciplined infantry, imposed the decisive strategic limit.
segments.question: If Charles XII had not been injured, could he have defeated the Russians at Moscow?
segments.answerShort: Probably not; he might have reached or bombarded Moscow, but Russian manpower and repeated relief armies made a sustainable siege or conquest unlikely.

evidence.note: The answer weighs a raid or bombardment against the infeasibility of a prolonged siege.

segments.title: Why Sweden invested in coastal warships
segments.summary: Sweden's coastal fleet reflected both limited manpower and a maritime empire whose Baltic waterways served as communications, defensive depth, and maneuver space.
segments.body: The Baltic connected Sweden's scattered imperial possessions and allowed forces to move along coasts and rivers. Shallow-draft warships exploited that geography while reducing some of the manpower and heavy-ship disadvantages Sweden faced against Russia. Coastal warfare was therefore not merely a compromise but a way to turn Sweden's environment into an operational advantage.
segments.question: Were Sweden's littoral ships a response to its coastline and lack of manpower compared with Russia?
segments.answerShort: Yes, partly, but they also exploited the Baltic as Sweden's internal communications network, defensive depth, and natural maneuver space.

evidence.note: The answer links manpower, coastline, maritime empire, and internal lines.

segments.title: How open-ocean and coastal sea conditions differ
segments.summary: Open water lets waves build more energy, while coastal waters break up swells but leave ships less room to recover from mistakes near rocks and land.
segments.body: The open ocean tends to produce larger, longer-developed swells because land does not interrupt them. A capable ship and crew can often ride those swells, whereas coastal waters introduce rocks, shoals, and limited maneuvering room. The coast may look calmer, but the consequences of navigation or handling errors can be more immediate.
segments.question: How is the middle of the ocean different from the edges?
segments.answerShort: Open-ocean waves can build larger without land interruption; coastal waters may break the swell but add rocks and much less room for error.

evidence.note: The answer compares wave development offshore with hazards and limited sea room near coasts.
